findings.description: safe_mean materializes the full iterable via list(values) with no size cap, so an attacker-controlled unbounded/huge stream causes unbounded memory consumption (CWE-770 uncontrolled resource allocation / DoS) — defense-in-depth only, as bounding input is properly the caller's responsibility for a pure library helper.

percentile calls sorted(values), fully materializing and O(n log n)-sorting an unbounded iterable; a hostile huge input drives memory/CPU exhaustion (CWE-770/CWE-400 DoS), again a caller-side bounding concern rather than a fixable flaw in this layer.

StreamingMedian.add uses insort into a Python list, which is O(n) per insertion (element shifting), so n adds are O(n^2) CPU and the list grows without bound — an attacker who can drive many add() calls causes quadratic-time/unbounded-memory DoS (CWE-407/CWE-770); the docstring documents the sorted-list tradeoff, so this is an accepted-design note, not a regression.

moving_average accepts NaN/Inf values (no float sanitization), letting a malicious input poison the rolling sum and propagate NaN across all subsequent windows via the incremental subtraction on line 51 — primarily a data-integrity/correctness concern with negligible security impact for an internal dashboard helper.
